narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 1.80 (well below the average of 3.21), LSAT score of 25 (far under the typical 36.70), and low decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 school grades (both at the 1st decile compared to averages around 5.5)—and calculated a 98% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This extremely high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize candidates more likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were, in order: your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking. Your bottom-of-the-class standing in Year 3 grades had the strongest negative impact, signaling to the model a consistent struggle with coursework that often correlates with bar exam challenges. Similarly, your low LSAT score reinforced this by showing weaker analytical skills compared to peers who typically score much higher. Even your Year 1 decile, while slightly less influential, further confirmed early academic difficulties that deviated sharply from the stronger starts of most admitted students. Your full-time enrollment status aligned with the vast majority (over 92%) of students, so it didn't sway the outcome much. Overall, your academic metrics stood out as much weaker than typical successful applicants, overriding other neutral factors like family income. While this is disappointing, especially given your determination to pursue law, it's not the end of the road. To improve your chances next time, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or a master's program if possible, and addressing any study habits that led to those low school deciles. Many students in similar situations have rebounded by targeting these areas—consider consulting a prep tutor or academic advisor tailored to law school admissions for a personalized plan forward. With targeted effort, you can shift the model's view of your potential.
